title: Unraveling and controlling the self-assembly pathways of cubic colloids
authors: Dillip Kumar Mohapatra, Teun W. J. Verouden, Janne-Mieke Meijer
abstract: The self-assembly of anisotropic building blocks into complex spatial architectures is an important design strategy in material science but the mechanisms by which the anisotropic interactions influence the early-stage growth and formation of disordered (non-)equilibrium structures remain poorly understood. Here, we experimentally demonstrate that tuning the strength of shape-induced directional bonds changes the self-assembly pathways of cubic colloids. By tracking the growth kinetics and internal reorganizations of small clusters at increasing attraction strength, we identify three self-assembly regimes: (i) nucleation and growth regime: slow reorganization-dominated growth of crystalline clusters, (ii) dynamic regime: diffusion-limited growth with dynamic cube reorganizations leading to disordered crystalline clusters and (iii) static regime: diffusion-limited growth of kinetically arrested clusters unable to reorganize due to directional bonding constraints. We further show that transitions between these regimes are reversible and allow pathway engineering to control the structure and disorder. Our results reveal how directional bonding governs pathway selection, providing important insights for the rational design of reconfigurable colloidal, nano-, and biomaterials.
summary: This paper experimentally maps out how tuning the strength of directional attractions allows researchers to control the self-assembly pathways of cubic particles. By navigating between nucleation, dynamic, and static regimes, the authors demonstrate a method for 'pathway engineering' to create specific colloidal superstructures.
main_problem: Understanding how the strength of shape-induced directional bonds influences the self-assembly pathways, growth kinetics, and structural formation of disordered and equilibrium structures in cubic colloids.
main_result: The study identifies three distinct self-assembly regimes (nucleation and growth, dynamic, and static) controlled by attraction strength and demonstrates that transitions between these regimes are reversible, enabling 'pathway engineering'.
method: Real-time particle-resolved tracking using Confocal Laser Scanning Microscopy (CLSM) to observe 2D self-assembly driven by temperature-tuned critical Casimir forces.
model_or_system: Micron-sized, hollow silica cubes in a binary mixture of lutidine and water, where short-range attractive critical Casimir forces are controlled via temperature changes.
key_observables: Fractal dimension (Df), cluster size distribution, bond correlation function, local packing symmetry (Square vs. Rhombic/Lambda1), and particle attachment/detachment probabilities.
important_parameters: Temperature difference from critical point (delta T), particle area fraction (phi_A), and interaction range.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes particles can be treated as having equal mass and focuses on the role of interaction strength and shape in crystallization and gelation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the cubic colloid shape, the temperature-dependent transition from repulsion to attraction, and the time evolution of self-assembly. Figure 2 visualizes specific motions like sliding and turning. Figure 3 presents the experimental state diagram, local packing transitions, and fractal dimension changes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of anisotropic self-assembly, describes the experimental platform and critical Casimir mechanism, details the identification of three growth regimes, demonstrates the reversibility of pathways (pathway engineering), and concludes with the implications for material design.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The self-assembly of cubic colloids into different clusters at different attraction strength. (a) Transmission electron microscopy image of hollow silica cubes. (b) Schematic illustration of the critical Casimir attraction between colloidal cubes in a lutidine water mixture controlled by the temperature difference with the critical point ∆T = TC −T that leads to different attraction strengths. (c) CLSM images obtained at different times during the self-assembly process of colloidal cube dispersions with an area fraction of ϕA = 0.21 at ∆T = 0.55 ◦C. (Insets) higher magnification of the observed cube clusters. (d) CLSM images of the final colloidal cube superstructures after 60 min...

FIG. 2. The growth and dynamics of cubic colloid clusters. (a) CLSM images of single cube cluster over time with detected particle positions for (top row, purple) ∆T = 0.55 ◦C, (middle row, yellow) ∆T = 0.50 ◦C, and (bottom row, green) ∆T = 0.25 ◦C. (b) Clusters at t = 60 min with detected particles positions colored according to bond angle fluctuations. (c) Schematic representation of observed cube dynamics, (i) attachment, (ii) detachment, (iii) sliding and (iv) turning. (d) Cluster size and (e) Shape parameter of each cluster in (a-b) over time. (f) Bulk growth and shrinking probability after a certain ∆t for clusters forming at (top, putple) ∆T = 0.55 ◦C and (bottom, blue) ∆T = 0.45 ◦C....

FIG. 3. Three self-assembly regimes identified by the analysis of the growth process and cube dynamics. (a) The observed local packing of the cube clusters at ∆T =0.45 ◦C after the full self-assembly process, with increasing particle area fraction, ϕA the lattice structure in the clusters switches from square, SQ, to rhombic, Λ1. (b) Fractal dimension of cube clusters Df that decreases with ∆T for all ϕA but at at different ∆T that directly correlates to the attraction strength. (c) Bond break probability Pb and bond correlation B at τ = 40τB for different ϕA showing that where cube detachment stops Pb(τ) < 0.05 and where cube dynamics cease B(τ) > 0.95 occur at different ∆T. (d)...

FIG. 4. The self-assembly pathway controls the superstructure morphology and size. (a) Different thermal pathways applied to a cube dispersion at ϕA = 0.15 with corresponding CLSM images of single clusters with detected particle positions colour-coded according to their local arrangement (red) Square-lattice, (blue) Λ1-lattice and (white) unassigned. (b) Time-lapse confocal images along AM showing reversible transitions between branched Λ1, square, and dense Λ1 states at 16 (I), 60 (II), 115 (III), and 153 min (IV) (ϕA = 0.15). Particles are colour-coded by local structure. (c) Temporal evolution of square and Λ1 fractions during the modulated cycle (AM). (d) Corresponding fractal...
